revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23989927232|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text: [[ar:فوشيمي إناري]][[az:Fuşimi İnari məbədi]][[bcl:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[cs:Fušimi Inari-taiša]][[de:Fushimi Inari-Taisha]][[el:Φουσίμι Ινάρι-τάισα]][[en:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[eo:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[es:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[eu:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[fa:معبد فوشیمی‌ایناری]][[fi:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[fr:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[he:מקדש פושימי אינארי]][[hu:Fusimi Inari-nagyszentély]][[id:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[is:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[it:Santuario di Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[ja:伏見稲荷大社]][[jv:Fushimi Inari Taisha]][[ka:ფუშიმი ინარი-ტაიშა]][[ko:후시미 이나리 신사]][[la:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[mk:Фушими Инари-таиша]][[my:ဖုရှိမိအိနရိ - တိုင်းရှ]][[nl:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[pl:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[pt:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[ru:Фусими-Инари-тайся]][[simple:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[sl:Fušimi Inari taiša]][[sv:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[ta:புசிமி இனாரி தையிசா]][[th:ศาลเจ้าฟูชิมิอินาริ]][[tr:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[vi:Fushimi Inari-taisha]][[zh-classical:伏見稻荷大社]][[zh:伏見稻荷大社]] {{short description|Shinto shrine near Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Fushimi Inari-taisha | native_name = 伏見稲荷大社 | native_name_lang = jp | image = File:Torii path with lantern at Fushimi Inari Taisha Shrine, Kyoto, Japan.jpg | caption = ''Torii'' path with a hanging lantern at Fushimi Inari-Taisha Shrine | map_type = Japan Kyoto#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|2|N|135|46|22|E|type:landmark|display=inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|qid=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} | deity = [[Ukanomitama|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Ōkami]], et al. as {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} | founded_by = | established = 711 | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = {{URL|http://inari.jp/en/}} | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Fushimi Inari-taisha'''|伏見稲荷大社|lead=yes}} is the head {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} of the ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}}, located in {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine sits at the base of a mountain, also named Inari, which is {{convert|233|m}} above sea level, and includes trails up the mountain to many smaller shrines which span {{convert|4|km}} and take approximately 2 hours to walk up.{{cite web|url=http://www.jisyameguri.com/chiiki/kyoto/fushimi-inari/ |script-title=ja:全国のお稲荷さんの総本宮、伏見稲荷大社を参拝しました。|access-date=28 March 2014|language=ja|trans-title=Nationwide Inari Shrines, I visited the Fushimi Inari-taisha.}} It is unclear whether the mountain's name, ''Inariyama'', or the shrine's name came first.Keller (2022): 2. Inari was originally and remains primarily the ''kami'' of rice and agriculture, but merchants also worship Inari as the patron of business.Keller (2022): 1. Each of Fushimi Inari-taisha's roughly 10,000 [[torii]] were donated by a Japanese business, and approximately 800 of these are set in a row to form the Senbon Torii, creating the impression of a tunnel.{{cite web|url=https://crd.ndl.go.jp/reference/modules/d3ndlcrdentry/index.php?page=ref_view&id=1000130073 |title=伏見稲荷大社にある千本鳥居の由来と数を知りたい|website=National Diet Library of Japan Collaborative Reference Database|access-date=26 March 2023}} The shrine is said to have ten thousand such gates in total that designate the entrance to the holy domain of ''kami'' and protect it against wicked forces. Owing to the popularity of Inari's [[bunrei|division]] and [[kanjō|re-enshrinement]], this shrine is said to have as many as 32,000 sub-shrines (分社 ''bunsha'') throughout Japan.{{cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776|title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|access-date=31 March 2010}} ==History== '']] The shrine gained imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} decreed that messengers carry written accounts of important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including the Inari Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Fushimi Inari-taisha was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. Unlike most Shinto shrines, Fushimi Inari-taisha, in keeping with typical Inari shrines, has an open view of the main object of worship (a mirror). A drawing in Kiyoshi Nozaki's ''Kitsune: Japan's Fox of Mystery, Romance and Humor'' in 1786 depicting the shrine says that its two-story entry gate was built by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. The shrine draws several million worshipers over the [[Japanese New Year]], 2.69 million for 3 days in 2006 reported by the police, the most in western Japan. ===Structures=== The earliest structures were built in 711 on the ''Inariyama'' hill in southwestern Kyoto, but the shrine was re-located in 816 on the request of the monk {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}. The main shrine structure was built in 1499.Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (1998). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&dq=1752+revolt++and+japon&pg=PA123 ''Japan encyclopedia,'' p. 224.] At the bottom of the hill are the {{nihongo|main gate|楼門||''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'', "tower gate"}} and the {{Nihongo|main shrine|御本殿||''go-honden''}}. Behind them, in the middle of the mountain, the {{Nihongo|inner shrine|奥宮||''okumiya''}} is reachable by a path lined with thousands of ''torii''. On the way to the top of the mountain are tens of thousands of rock altars ''(otsuka'' お塚) for private worship. These rock altars are personalised Inari that have been set up there by citizen. Most of them have individual names for Inari engraved on them.Smyers (1996): 93-94. ==== Senbon Torii ==== The highlight of the shrine is the rows of [[torii]] gates, known as {{nowrap|Senbon Torii.}} The custom to donate a torii began spreading from the Edo period (1603–1868) to have a wish come true or in gratitude for a wish that came true, with successive gates being added up to the present day by donors out of gratitude. Along the main path there are around 800 ''[[torii]]'' gates. ==Access and environs== The shrine is just outside {{ill|Inari Station|en|Inari Station|qid=Q510674|lt=Inari Station|1=Inari Station}} on the [[:en:Nara Line|Nara Line]] of the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} (JR), a five-minute ride from [[:en:Kyoto Station|Kyoto Station]]. It is a short walk from {{ill|Fushimi-Inari Station|en|Fushimi-Inari Station|qid=Q2153247|lt=Fushimi-Inari Station|1=Fushimi-Inari Station}} on the {{ill|Keihan Main Line|en|Keihan Main Line|qid=Q2598752|lt=Main Line|1=Keihan Main Line}} of the {{ill|Keihan Electric Railway|en|Keihan Electric Railway|qid=Q1188274|lt=Keihan Electric Railway|1=Keihan Electric Railway}}.[http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3915.html Fushimi Inari Shrine], How to get there The shrine is open 24 hours with the approach to the shrine and the {{nihongo|[[Honden]]|本殿|main hall}} itself illuminated all night. There is no entrance fee. In the approach to the shrine are a number of sweet shops selling {{Nihongo||辻占煎餅|[[:en:Tsujiura|Tsujiura]] {{ill|Senbei|en|Senbei|qid=Q198788|lt=Senbei|1=Senbei}}}}, a form of {{ill|Fortune cookie|en|Fortune cookie|qid=Q782794|lt=Fortune cookie|1=Fortune cookie}} dating at least to the 19th century, and which are believed by some to be the origin of the American fortune cookie.{{ill|Jennifer 8. Lee|en|Jennifer 8. Lee|qid=Q702510|lt=Lee, Jennifer 8|1=Jennifer 8. Lee}}. (January 16, 2008). "[https://www.nytimes.com/2008/01/16/dining/16fort.html Solving a Riddle Wrapped in a Mystery Inside a Cookie]" ''{{ill|The New York Times|en|The New York Times|qid=Q9684|lt=The New York Times|1=The New York Times}}''. Retrieved on January 16, 2008.{{cite web|title=Fortune Cookies are really from Japan. |url=http://www.fortunecookiechronicles.com/2008/01/16/fortune-cookies-are-really-from-japan/ |author-link=Jennifer 8. Lee |first=Jennifer |last=8. Lee |date=January 16, 2008 |work=[[The Fortune Cookie Chronicles]] official website |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110725122139/http://www.fortunecookiechronicles.com/2008/01/16/fortune-cookies-are-really-from-japan/ |archive-date=2011-07-25 }}{{cite web|last=Ono|first=Gary|title=Japanese American Fortune Cookie: A Taste of Fame or Fortune – Part II|url=http://www.discovernikkei.org/forum/en/node/1935|date=2007-10-31|access-date=2020-02-16|archive-date=2009-04-04|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090404074145/http://www.discovernikkei.org/forum/en/node/1935|url-status=dead}} ==In popular culture== {{prose|date=June 2017}} *''{{ill|Memoirs of a Geisha (film)|en|Memoirs of a Geisha (film)|qid=Q45839|lt=Memoirs of a Geisha|1=Memoirs of a Geisha (film)}}'' (2005) *''{{ill|Aria (manga)|en|Aria (manga)|qid=Q91802|lt=Aria the Natural ep. 5|1=Aria (manga)}}'' (2006) *''{{ill|Inari, Konkon, Koi Iroha|en|Inari, Konkon, Koi Iroha|qid=Q429161|lt=Inari, Konkon, Koi Iroha|1=Inari, Konkon, Koi Iroha}}'' (2010) *''{{ill|Rurouni Kenshin|en|Rurouni Kenshin|qid=Q216439|lt=Rurouni Kenshin|1=Rurouni Kenshin}}'', site of [[:en:Makoto Shishio|Makoto Shishio]]'s base *''{{ill|Kamen Rider Fourze|en|Kamen Rider Fourze|qid=Q1343566|lt=Kamen Rider Fourze|1=Kamen Rider Fourze}} {{ill|List of Kamen Rider Fourze episodes|en|List of Kamen Rider Fourze episodes|qid=Q6575615|lt=ep. 33|1=List of Kamen Rider Fourze episodes}}'' (2012) *''{{ill|Samsara (2011 film)|en|Samsara (2011 film)|qid=Q3150366|lt=Samsara (2011 film)|1=Samsara (2011 film)}}'' (2011) *''{{ill|The Quintessential Quintuplets|en|The Quintessential Quintuplets|qid=Q38277250|lt=The Quintessential Quintuplets|1=The Quintessential Quintuplets}}'' (2017) *''High School Inari Tamamo-chan'', where the main character a fox spirit came from along with her siblings. A part of the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play ''{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|qid=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji|1=Kokaji}}'' takes place in ''Fushimi Inari-taisha''.{{cite web |url=http://www.noh-kyogen.com/story/english/Kokaji.pdf |title=Kokaji (pamphlet) |author= |publisher=noh-kyogen.com |access-date=2018-01-09 |archive-date=2018-08-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820173916/http://www.noh-kyogen.com/story/english/Kokaji.pdf |url-status=dead }} The shrine inspired {{ill|Nintendo|en|Nintendo|qid=Q8093|lt=Nintendo|1=Nintendo}} game designer, {{ill|Shigeru Miyamoto|en|Shigeru Miyamoto|qid=Q12382|lt=Shigeru Miyamoto|1=Shigeru Miyamoto}}, to create the series {{ill|Star Fox|en|Star Fox|qid=Q1501747|lt=Star Fox|1=Star Fox}}. In the series, players control {{ill|Fox McCloud|en|Fox McCloud|qid=Q2081430|lt=Fox McCloud|1=Fox McCloud}} and fly starfighters through colorful rings in [[:en:Aerial combat|Aerial combat]]. Miyamoto attributed these inspirations to the Fushimi Inari Shrine, which is within walking distance of the Nintendo Kyoto campus.{{cite web | url=https://iwataasks.nintendo.com/interviews/3ds/starfox/0/2/ | title=Iwata Asks - Nintendo 3DS - Page 3 }} ==Gallery== File:Fushimi-Inari-Shrine-Senbon-Torii-2018-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of the south-western wing of Senbon Torii path. File:Fushimi-Inari-Shrine-Senbon-Torii-2016-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of the north-eastern wing of Senbon Torii path. File:Toris inari 3.jpg|A ''[[torii]]'' path across the mountain from the side File:Hushimi-inari-taisha honden.jpg|A honden File:Kyoto FushimiInari01.jpg|The main gate File:Fushimi-Inari-taisha 4.jpg File:20100714 Kyoto Fushimi Inari 1655.jpg File:Kyoto Schrein Fushimi-Inari-taisha 10.jpg File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg ===Fox=== Foxes (''{{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|qid=Q692111|lt=Kitsune|1=Kitsune}}''), regarded as the messengers, are often found in Inari shrines. One attribute is a key (for the rice granary) in their mouths. {{clear}} File:Inari-kitsune fox in front of an Inari shrine with a key in its mouth.jpg|Fox holding a key in its mouth, at the main gate of the Fushimi Inari shrine File:The image of the fox that example mouth the jewel.jpg|Fox holding a jewel in its mouth at the main gate of the Fushimi Inari shrine File:Inari sculpture.jpg|Fox sculpture in Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine File:Inari fountain.jpg|Fox fountain in Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine File:Fox Fountain - Fushimi Inari 2010.jpg|Another view of the fox fountain File:20181110 Fushimi Inari shrine 9.jpg|Fox altar in Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine File:Fushimi Inari Taisha Kitsune.jpg|Kitsune statue in the [[:en:Senbon Torii|Senbon Torii]] File:Fushimi_Inari_shrines.jpg|Fox guardian at the Fushimi Inari shrine. ==See also== {{Portal|Architecture|Japan}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} ==References== {{reflist}} ===Bibliography=== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * Keller, Matthew Paul (2022): ''The Appeal of the Fox: The Cult of Inari and Premodern Japan.'' University of Southern California. * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (1998). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&dq=1752+revolt++and+japon&pg=PA123 ''Japan encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|qid=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Smyers, Karen A. (1997). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140928091422/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2641 Inari pilgrimage: Following one’s path on the mountain], Japanese Journal of Religious Studies 24 (3–4), 427–452 * Smyers, Karen A. (1996): "My Own Inari": Personalization of the deity in Inari Worship. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies. 23 (1-2): 85-116. * Cali, Joseph, and John Dougill. Shinto shrines: a guide to the sacred sites of Japan's ancient religion. University of Hawaii Press, 2012. ==External links== {{commons Category|Fushimi Inari-taisha}} *[http://inari.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://inari.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.taleofgenji.org/fushimi-inari.html Photographs of Fushimi Inari-taisha] on taleofgenji.org *[https://www.accessible-japan.com/places/japan/kyoto/kyoto/attractions/fushimi-inari-shrine/ Accessibility information] on Accessible Japan *{{OSM|w|96291583}} {{Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|state=expanded}} {{Inari Faith}} {{coord|34|58|02|N|135|46|22|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[File:Nobu3withfoxy IMG 0155 (14564801685).jpg|thumb|Front view of the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]] [[File:Fushimi Inari-taisha Senbon torii - one section - 2015-12-29.webm|thumb|left|160px|(video) Walking up part of the torii path.]] [[en:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[ar:فوشيمي إناري]] [[az:Fuşimi İnari məbədi]] [[cs:Fušimi Inari-taiša]] [[de:Fushimi Inari-Taisha]] [[el:Φουσίμι Ινάρι-τάισα]] [[eo:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[es:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[eu:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[fa:معبد فوشیمی‌ایناری]] [[fi:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[fr:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[he:מקדש פושימי אינארי]] [[hu:Fusimi Inari-nagyszentély]] [[id:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[is:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[it:Santuario di Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[ja:伏見稲荷大社]] [[jv:Fushimi Inari Taisha]] [[ka:ფუშიმი ინარი-ტაიშა]] [[ko:후시미 이나리 신사]] [[la:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[mk:Фушими Инари-таиша]] [[my:ဖုရှိမိအိနရိ - တိုင်းရှ]] [[nl:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[pl:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[pt:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[ru:Фусими-Инари-тайся]] [[simple:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[sl:Fušimi Inari taiša]] [[sv:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[ta:புசிமி இனாரி தையிசா]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าฟูชิมิอินาริ]] [[tr:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[vi:Fushimi Inari-taisha]] [[zh:伏見稻荷大社]] [[zh-classical:伏見稻荷大社]] {{wikidata link|Q714828}} [[Category:Former Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]
